text.title: Defaults and constraints applied to all subagents
text.summary: A small, strict set of defaults applies to every subagent so that no single phase can leak destructive or out-of-band side effects.

list.item: No remote `git push` without an explicit user instruction in the run.
No skipping git hooks (`--no-verify`, `--no-gpg-sign`). When a hook fails, fix the underlying issue and re-commit.
No bulk `git add -A` / `git add .` — stage explicit paths. Bulk staging silently picks up local-only state and secrets.
No commits to local-only files (preview data, dev toggles, environment overrides) unless the feature spec explicitly requires it.
No destructive operations against shared environments (DB writes, queue triggers, schema changes) without explicit user authorization.
Move the feature folder through the SDD lifecycle: `todo/ → in-progress/ → done/`.
Move each task file through the same lifecycle inside the feature folder.
Update an append-only release notes / changelog file per commit when the project requires one (pre-commit hook usually enforces this).
Use a 50-character commit-title convention with a stable prefix shape (`: ` or `: `). The exact convention is documented in `reference/git-conventions.md`.

section.title: Sensitive operations require confirmation
section.p: Operations that change shared state must round-trip through the user before execution. The orchestrator confirms via the `AskUserQuestion` primitive; the deployer subagent additionally verifies a confirmation token that the orchestrator threads through.

list.item: Pushing any branch to a remote.
Deploying artifacts to any non-local environment.
Running data-mutating jobs (DAG triggers, reimports, batch updates).
Updating tracker statuses to states that imply downstream notifications.

rule.statement: If a target environment label is ambiguous (e.g., the user says "qa" but two qa flavours exist), the orchestrator MUST confirm the exact target with `AskUserQuestion` before any deploy or destructive op.
rule.rationale: Wrong-environment deploys are silent until they cause incidents. A two-second confirmation is always cheaper than a rollback.
